title: 第 19 節
en: This is the story I wished to tell you to-day. And though it may have sounded to some of you like a fairy-tale, believe me it is truer in all its details than many a chapter of contemporary history.

As I find that some of my remarks as to the handing down of the ancient Sanskrit literature by means of oral tradition, and the permanence of that system to the present day have been received with a certain amount of incredulity, I subjoin some extracts from the Rig-veda-pra'tisakhya, to show how the oral teaching of the Vedas was carried on at least 500 B.C., and some statements from the pen of two native scholars, to show how it is maintained to the present day.

The Pratisakhya of the Rig- Veda, of which I published the text and a German translation in 1856, contains the rules according to which the sacred texts are to be pronounced. I still ascribe this, which seems to me the oldest Pratisakhya, to the 5th or 6th century B.C., to a period between Yaska on one side, and Pamni on the other, until more powerful arguments can be brought forward against this date than have been hitherto advanced. In the 1 5th chapter of that Pratisakhya we find a description of the method followed in the schools of ancient India. The teacher, we are told, must himself have passed through the recognised cur riculum, and have fulfilled all the duties of a Brahmanical student (brahmaMrin), before he is allowed to become a teacher, and he must teach such students only who submit to all the rules of studentship. He should settle down in a proper place. If he has only one pupil or two, they should sit on his right side ; if more, they must sit as there is room for them. At the beginning of each lecture the 160 LECTURE I EL pupils embrace the feet of their teacher, and say: Kead, Sir. The teacher answers : Cm, Yes, and then pronounces two words, or, if it is a compound, one. When the teacher has pronounced one word or two, the first pupil repeats the first word, but if there is anything that requires explanation, the pupil says Sir ; and after it has been explained to him (the teacher says), Om, Yes, Sir.

In this manner they go on till they have finished a prasna (question), which consists of three verses, or, if they are verses of more than forty to fortytwo syllables, of two verses. If they are panktiverses of forty to forty-two syllables each, a prasna may comprise either two or three ; and if a hymn consists of one verse only, that is supposed to form a prasna. After the prasna is finished, they have all to repeat it once more, and then to go on learning it by heart, pronouncing every syllable with the high accent. After the teacher has first told a prasna to his pupil on the right, the others go round him to the right, and this goes on till the whole adhyaya or lecture is finished ; a lecture consisting generally of sixty prasnas. At the end of the last half-verse the teacher says Sir, and the pupil replies, Om, Yes, Sir, repeating also the verses required at the end of a lecture. The pupils then embrace the feet of their teacher, and are dismissed.

These are the general features of a lesson, but the Pratisakhya contains a number of minute rules be sides. For instance, in order to prevent small words from being neglected, the teacher is to repeat twice every word which has but one high accent, or consists of one vowel only. A number of small words are to be followed by the particle iti, thus ;
THE ANCIENT LITERATURE OF INDIA. 161 others are to be followed by iti, and then to be repeated again, e. g. &a-iti &a.

These lectures continued during about half the year, the term beginning generally with the rainy season. There were, however, many holidays on which no lectures were given, and on these points also the most minute regulations are given both in the Gr&hva and Dharma-sutras.
This must suffice as a picture of what took place in India about 500 B.C. Let us now see what remains of the ancient system at present.
In a letter received from the learned editor of the ' ShacMarsana-Hntanika,' or Studies in Indian Philo sophy, dated Poona, 8 June, 1878, the writer says :

'A student of a Hig-Veda-sakha (a recension of the Rig- Veda), if sharp and assiduous, takes about eight years to learn the Dasagranthas, the ten books, which consist of (1) The Samhit4, or the hymns.
(2) The Br4hmawa, the prose treatise on sacri fices, etc.
(3) The Aranyaka, the forest-book.
(4) The Grihya-sutras, the rules on domestic ceremonies.
(5-10) The six Angas, treatises on $iksha, pronunciation, 6ryotisha, astronomy, Kalpa, cere monial, Vyakara^a, grammar, Nighawfu and Nirukta, etymology, Khandas, metre.

' A pupil studies every day during the eight years, except on the holidays, the so-called anadhyaya, i. e. non-reading days. There being 360 days in a lunar year, the eight years would give him 2880 days. From this 384 holidays have to be deducted, leaving him 2496 work-days during the eight years.
' Now the ten books consist on a rough calculation of 29,500 slokas, so that a student of the KigVeda has to learn about twelve slokas a day, a sloka consisting of thirty-two syllables.

' I ought to point out to you the source of my information. We have an association in Poona which is called the Vedasastrotte^akasabha, which annually awards prizes in all recognised branches of Sanskrit learning, such as the six schools of Indian philosophy, the Alankara-sastra or rhetoric, Vaidyaka or medi cine, 6ryotisha or astronomy, recitation of the Veda in its different forms, such as Pada, Krama, Ghana, and Gats,, and all the subjects I have already men tioned under the name of Dasagrantha, in the case of the Kig-veda Brahmans. The prize-men are recommended by a board of examiners. In every subject a threefold test is employed, — theoretical knowledge of the subject (prakriya), general know ledge of the subject (upasthiti), and the construction of passages from recognised works in each branch of knowledge (grantharthapariksha). About 1000 rupees are distributed by the leading native gentle men of Poona. At a meeting held the 8th May last there were about fifty Sanskrit Pandits and Vaidikas. In their presence I got the information from an old Vaidika much respected in Poona/ Another interesting account of the state of native learning comes from the pen of Professor K. G. Bhandarkar, M. A. ('Indian Antiquary/ 1874, p. 132) :

to the study of a particular Veda, and a particular sakha (recension) of a Veda ; and the domestic rites of the family are performed according to the ritual described in the Sutra connected with that Veda.

THE ANCIENT LITERATURE OF INDIA. 163 The study consists in getting by heart the books forming the particular Veda. In Northern India, where the predominant Veda is the White Ya^ush, and the sakha that of the Madhyandinas, this study has almost died out, except at Banaras, where Brahmanic families from all parts of India are settled. It prevails to some extent in Gujarat, but to a much greater extent in the Mara^M country ; and in Tailangana there is a large number of Brahmans who still devote their life to this study. Numbers of these go about to all parts of the country in search of dakshma (fee, alms), and all well-to-do natives patronize them according to their means, by getting them to repeat portions of their Veda, which is mostly the Black Ya^ush, with Apastamba for their Sutra. Hardly a week passes here in Bombay in which no Tailangana Brahman comes to me to ask for dakshiwa. On each occasion I get the men to repeat what they have learned, and compare it with the printed texts in my possession.

'With reference to their occupation, Brahmans of each Veda are generally divided into two classes, Grihasthas and Bhikshukas. The former devote themselves to a worldly avocation, while the latter spend their time in the study of their sacred books and the practice of their religious rites.

' Both these classes have to repeat daily the Sandhya-vandana or twilight-prayers, the forms of which are somewhat different for the different Vedas. But the repetition of the Gayatri-mantra ' Tat Savitur varer^yam,' etc., five, ten, twenty-eight, or a hundred and eight times, which forms the principal portion of the ceremony, is common to all.
'Besides this, a great many perform daily what M 2 164 LECTURE III.

is called Brahmaya^a, which on certain occasions is incumbent on all. This for the Kig-vedis consists of the first hymn of the first ma^ala, and the open ing sentences of the Aitareya Brahmawa, the five parts of the Aitareya Aranyaka, the Ya^/us-samhita, the Sama-samhita, the Atharva-sawhita", Asvalayana Kalpa Sutra, Nirukta, Khandas, Nigha?i£u, 6ryotisha, £iksh&, Pamni, Ysk/navalkya Smriti, Mahabharata, and the Sutras of Kawada, 6raimini, and Badaraya^a.
' Such Bhikshukas, however, as have studied the whole Veda repeat more than the first hymn ; they repeat as much as they wish (sa yavan manyeta tavad adhitya, Asvalayana).
' Some of the Bhikshukas are what are called Ya/mikas. They follow a priestly occupation, and are skilled in the performance of sacred rites . . ., .

' But a more important class of Bhikshukas are the Vaidikas, some of whom are Yac/nikas as well. Learning the Vedas by heart and repeating them in a manner never to make a single mistake, even in the accents, is the occupation of their life. The best Rig-vedi Vaidika knows by heart the Samhita, Pada, Krama, G&t& and Ghana of the hymns, the Aitareya Brahmawa and Ara^yaka, the Kalpa and Grihya Sutra of Asvalayana, the Nigha??£u, Nirukta, AViandas, (ryotisha, $iksha", and Pamni's grammar. A Vaidika is thus a living Vedic library.
' The Samhita, Pada, Krama, 6ra£a and Ghana are different names for peculiar arrangements of the text of the hymns.
to the phonetic rules peculiar to Sanskrit.
' In the Pada text the words are divided, and compounds also are dissolved.
THE ANCIENT LITERATURE OF INDIA. 165 ' In the Krama text, suppose we have a line of eleven words, they are arranged as follows, the rules of Sandhi being observed throughout for letters and accent :
zh: 這就是我今天想告訴諸位的故事。儘管在座有些人聽來或許像童話，但請相信我，它在所有細節上都比許多當代歷史篇章更為真實。
我發現，關於古代梵語文獻透過口頭傳統傳承、以及該體系持續至今的若干論述，曾引起一定程度的懷疑。因此，我附上《梨俱吠陀‧音韻學》（Rig-veda-pra'tisakhya）的若干摘錄，以說明吠陀的口頭教學至少在公元前五百年便已進行；同時也引述兩位本土學者筆下的陳述，以顯示該傳統如何維持至今。

《梨俱吠陀》的《波羅提娑佉》（Pratisakhya）——我於1856年出版了其原文與德文譯本——記載了誦讀聖典時應遵循的規則。我仍將這部在我看來最古老的《波羅提娑佉》歸屬於西元前五或六世紀，即介於耶斯卡（Yaska）與波你尼（Pāṇini）之間的時期，除非日後能提出比迄今為止更有力的論據來推翻這個年代。在該《波羅提娑佉》的第十五章中，我們找到了對古印度學派所遵循方法的描述。據記載，教師本人必須先完成公認的課程，並履行婆羅門學生（brahmacārin）的一切義務，才能獲準成為教師；而且他只能教導那些遵守所有學徒規矩的學生。他應在適當地點定居下來。若只有一兩名學生，他們應坐在他的右側；若人數更多，則須依空間許可而坐。每次授課開始時，學生們擁抱教師的雙足，說道：「請誦讀，先生。」教師回答：「Om，是的。」然後念出兩個詞，若為複合詞則念一個。教師唸完一兩個詞後，第一位學生複誦第一個詞；但若有需要解釋之處，學生便說：「先生。」待教師為他解釋完畢後（教師說）：「Om，是的，先生。」

以此方式，他們持續進行，直至完成一個 *prasna*（提問），該提問由三頌組成；若每頌超過四十至四十二音節，則由兩頌組成。若為每頌四十至四十二音節的 *pankti* 頌（五行頌），則一個 *prasna* 可包含兩頌或三頌；若一首讚歌僅由一頌構成，則該頌即被視為一個 *prasna*。完成 *prasna* 後，他們須全體再複誦一次，隨後繼續以高音調逐音節背誦。教師先向右側的弟子傳授一個 *prasna*，其餘弟子則繞行至其右側，如此反覆進行，直至整部 *adhyaya*（章節）或課程結束；一課通常包含六十個 *prasna*。在最後半頌的結尾，教師說「先生」，弟子回應「Om，是的，先生」，並同時複誦課程結束時所需的頌文。隨後，弟子們擁抱教師的雙足，而後解散。
這些是一堂課的一般特徵，但《普拉提沙基亞》還包含許多細微的規則。例如，為了防止小詞被忽略，教師應重複兩次每個只有一個高重音、或僅由一個元音構成的詞。若干小詞後面應接助詞 *iti*，如此一來。
其他詞語後應接 *iti*，然後再重複一次，例如：&a-iti &a。
這些講課持續約半年，學期通常始於雨季。然而，其中有許多假日是不上課的，關於這些細節，無論在《家居經》（Gṛhya-sūtra）或《法經》（Dharma-sūtra）中，都有極其詳盡的規定。
這足以作為一幅描繪西元前五百年左右在印度發生之事的圖景。現在，讓我們來看看這古老體系在當今留下了什麼。
在來自《ShacMarsana-Hntanika》（即《印度哲學研究》）博學編輯的一封信中，日期為1878年6月8日，寄自浦那，來信者寫道：
「一位研習梨俱吠陀學派（*Rig-Veda-sakha*，即《梨俱吠陀》之校訂本）的學生，若天資敏銳且勤勉不懈，約需八年時間學習《十書》（*Dasagranthas*），此十書包含：(1) 本集（*Samhitā*），亦即讚歌。
(2) 梵書（Brāhmaṇa），即關於祭祀等事宜的散文論著。
(3) The Aranyaka, the forest-book. (3) 森林書（Aranyaka），即「森林之書」。
(4) **Gṛhya-sūtras**（家庭儀軌經），即關於家庭儀式的規則。
(5-10) 六支（Ṣaḍaṅga），分別是：śikṣā（發音學）、jyotiṣa（天文學）、kalpa（儀軌學）、vyākaraṇa（文法學）、nirukta（語源學）、chandas（韻律學）。
' 學生在八年期間每日研讀，除節假日外——即所謂的「anadhyāya」（非誦讀日）。若以陰曆年三百六十日計算，八年共計兩千八百八十日。從中須扣除三百八十四個節假日，剩餘兩千四百九十六個工作日。
現今這十卷書粗略計算約有兩萬九千五百頌（śloka），因此研習《梨俱吠陀》的學生每日須學習約十二頌，每頌由三十二音節組成。

我應當向各位說明我的資訊來源。在浦那有一個名為「吠陀經義闡發會」（Vedasastrotte^akasabha）的協會，每年會針對梵學各公認領域頒發獎項，例如印度哲學六派、莊嚴論（Alankara-sastra）或修辭學、醫方明（Vaidyaka）、天文明（6ryotisha），以及以不同形式誦讀吠陀（如連聲誦（Pada）、層遞誦（Krama）、密集誦（Ghana）、合誦（Gats）），還有我在梨俱吠陀婆羅門案例中已提及的所有屬於「十部」（Dasagrantha）的科目。獲獎者由評審委員會推薦。每個科目都採用三重測試：該科目的理論知識（prakriya）、該科目的通識知識（upasthiti），以及從各知識領域公認著作中選取段落進行建構（grantharthapariksha）。約一千盧比由浦那當地主要的士紳們分發。在去年五月八日舉行的一場會議上，約有五十位梵文班智達與吠陀學者出席。在他們面前，我從一位在浦那備受敬重的年長吠陀學者那裡獲得了這項資訊。」另一位關於本土學術狀況的有趣記述，來自K. G. 班達卡教授（M. A.）的筆下（《印度古物誌》，1874年，第132頁）：
> 針對特定一部吠陀及其特定的 *śākhā*（傳本）進行研讀；而家庭的日常儀式，則依照與該吠陀相關的《經書》（Sūtra）所述之儀軌來執行。

此項研習在於將構成特定吠陀的典籍背誦牢記。在北印度，以白耶柔吠陀（White Yajurveda）為主、且學派為摩陀揚迪納（Madhyandina）之處，這項研習幾乎已絕跡，唯有在貝拿勒斯（Banaras）——來自印度各地的婆羅門家族聚居之地——尚存。在古吉拉特（Gujarat）地區，此風尚有一定程度流傳，但在馬拉塔（Maratha）地區更為盛行；而在泰倫加納（Tailangana），仍有大量婆羅門終生致力於此研習。他們當中許多人走遍全國各地，尋求*答剎希那*（dakshinā，酬金、佈施），所有富裕的本地人則按其財力資助他們，請他們誦讀其吠陀的段落——這些吠陀多為黑耶柔吠陀（Black Yajurveda），並以阿帕斯坦巴（Āpastamba）為其經書（Sūtra）。在孟買這裡，幾乎每週都有泰倫加納婆羅門前來向我請求*答剎希那*。每次我都請這些人背誦他們所學的內容，並與我手邊的印刷文本進行比對。
就其職業而言，每部《吠陀》的婆羅門通常分為兩類：**居家者**（Grihasthas）與**乞士**（Bhikshukas）。前者投身於世俗的謀生之道，後者則將時間用於研習聖典與履行宗教儀式。
' 這兩類人都必須每日重複誦唸 Sandhya-vandana（即晨昏祈禱文），其形式因不同吠陀而略有差異。但重複 Gayatri-mantra（即「Tat Savitur varer^yam」等咒語）五遍、十遍、二十八遍或一百零八遍，構成此儀式的主要部分，則是所有派別共通的。
除此之外，有許多人每日進行著……（M 2 164）

稱為 Brahmayaṇa，在某些場合是所有信徒都必須履行的。對梨俱吠陀學者而言，這包括第一曼陀羅的第一首讚歌、Aitareya Brahmaṇa 的開場語句、Aitareya Araṇyaka 的五個部分、Yajus-saṃhitā、Sāma-saṃhitā、Atharva-saṃhitā、Āśvalāyana Kalpa Sūtra、Nirukta、Chandas、Nighaṇṭu、Jyotiṣa、Śikṣā、Pāṇini、Yājñavalkya Smṛti、Mahābhārata，以及 Kaṇāda、Jaimini 與 Bādarāyaṇa 的經書。
> 然而，那些研讀過整部《吠陀》的比丘（Bhikshukas），會重複誦唸超過第一首讚歌；他們隨心所欲地重複誦唸（sa yavan manyeta tavad adhitya, Asvalayana）。
> 有些比丘（Bhikshukas）屬於所謂的夜摩迦（Yā/mikas）。他們從事祭司職務，並精通神聖儀式的執行……。

' 但更重要的一類比丘（Bhikshuka）是吠陀學者（Vaidika），其中有些人同時也是祭祀者（Yācñika）。終其一生，他們以背誦《吠陀》（Veda）並以絕不出錯的方式（連音調亦不例外）反覆誦唸為職志。最優秀的梨俱吠陀學者（Rig-vedi Vaidika）能熟記讚歌的《本集》（Saṃhitā）、《詞彙》（Pada）、《連環》（Krama）、《歌詠》（Gāna）與《密集》（Ghana），以及《愛達羅氏梵書》（Aitareya Brāhmaṇa）與《森林書》（Āraṇyaka）、阿濕瓦羅延（Āśvalāyana）的《儀軌經》（Kalpa Sūtra）與《家宅經》（Gṛhya Sūtra），還有《尼犍豆》（Nighaṇṭu）、《尼祿多》（Nirukta）、《闍陀》（Chandas）、《天文》（Jyotiṣa）、《音聲》（Śikṣā）與波你尼（Pāṇini）的語法書。因此，一位吠陀學者（Vaidika）便是一座活生生的吠陀圖書館。
『Saṃhitā、Pada、Krama、Jaṭā 與 Ghana 是頌詩文本特殊編排方式的不同名稱。』
根據梵文特有的語音規則。
在《 Pada 文本》中，詞彙被劃分開來，複合詞亦被拆解。
在 Krama 文本中，假設我們有一行由十一個詞組成的句子，其排列方式如下，且全程遵循連音（Sandhi）規則處理字母與重音：
